data.title: Honeywell: Price Pullback Is A Great Opportunity
data.description:
Hunting for stock bargains can be a highly rewarding endeavor. However, looking only at stocks with low PE ratios can cause one to miss out on some great long-term wealth compounders. These are what I would call 'set it and forget it' types of stocks that ought to be accumulated when they are reasonably priced. That's because these companies have moat-worthy businesses that rarely, if ever, result in a 'cheap' valuation. Bottom of Form

Such I find the case to be with Honeywell International (NASDAQ:HON), which I last covered back in April, highlighting an opportune time to buy the stock, its focus on energy security and digitalization, and long-term growth prospects in aerospace and automation. That turned out to be good timing, as HON has delivered a 19% total return for investors since my last piece.

The total return would have been higher had it not been for the recent dip in price from $240 to $222. While HON isn't cheap, it's far from being expensive, either, with a forward PE of 21. In this article, I revisit HON, including recent business fundamentals, and discuss why the recent pullback in price presents a solid entry point for this GARP (growth at a reasonable price) stock, so let's get started!

Why HON?

Honeywell International is a diversified industrial conglomerate. It operates in four key segments of Aerospace Technologies (commercial, defense, and space), Industrial Automation (process solutions, warehouse automations, sensing technologies), Building Automation (HVAC, energy management), and Energy & Sustainability (advanced materials, sustainable fuels).

Notably, management plans to unlock shareholder value, similar to what industrial peer GE (GE) did, by spinning off its Advanced Materials and Aerospace segments in two separate transactions over the next 12 months. This creates 3 distinct entities and enables RemainCo to focus on its Automation core competency across the Industrial and Building sectors.

This could help HON to create shareholder value, as the three standalone businesses could be worth more than the sum of the parts. As shown below, HON had a decent track record of beating the S&P 500 (SPY) in total return over the past 10 years until 2023, when it started to lag the overall market.

Honeywell demonstrated respectable results during Q2 2025, with organic sales rising by 5% YoY, which was above the high end of guidance range. This was led by robust growth in Building Automation, which saw its third consecutive quarter of 8% sales growth. As shown below, all segments except for Industrial Automation saw growth in the 6–8% range during the second quarter.

More encouraging is HON's higher profit growth (compared to revenue growth) of 8% YoY, due to segment margin expansion in the faster growing Building and Industrial Automation segments. This resulted in robust adjusted EPS growth of 10% YoY, exceeding the upper end of management's guidance by 5 cents. Notably, HON increased R&D spend in all business segments, with it rising to 4.6% of sales. This upfront investment is designed to accelerate innovation-led growth in future periods.

Management raised its full-year revenue growth guidance from 3% previously to 4.5% at the midpoint of range and adjusted EPS growth of 7% to $10.55 at the midpoint of range. This is driven by management's expectations for segment margin growth of 50 basis points over the prior year. Despite higher tariffs and ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties, HON expects pricing productivity and alternative sourcing strategies to mitigate those impacts.

Near-term growth is supported by bolt-on acquisitions like the $1.8 billion purchase of Johnson Matthey's Catalyst Technologies business to enhance the UOP segment (energy solutions business). This transaction gives HON additional capabilities in sustainable methanol, sustainable aviation fuel, hydrogen, and ammonia to serve its customer base.

Another acquisition is Li-ion Tamer, which enhances HON's building automation capabilities in the high-growth energy and data center markets. Building automation has a sizable $100+ billion addressable market globally, and that's expected to grow at a robust 8.8% CAGR between now and the end of this decade. Additionally, the Industrial Automation market is expected to grow at a 9% CAGR through 2032 to $493 billion, as shown below.

HON is supported by a strong balance sheet with an 'A' credit rating from S&P. It has a very high cash and short-term investment balance of $10.7 billion and a safe net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio of 2.7x. The stock currently yields 2%, and the dividend is well covered by a 43% payout ratio. It also comes with a five-year dividend CAGR of 4.9% and 5 consecutive years of growth.

Admittedly, HON's yield isn't high, but it also returns capital to shareholders through stock buybacks, making it a total return play. As shown below, HON has reduced nearly 10% of its outstanding float over the past 5 years.

Lastly, while HON isn't cheap, it's far from being expensive, either, at the current price of $222 with a forward PE of 21.0. This is considering potential value creation opportunities from the planned spinoff of the Advanced Materials business later this year and the Aerospace spin-off planned for the second half of next year.

If the success of the GE spin-offs is of any indication, investors could see meaningful gains given the unique growth trajectories of each business. In particular, HON's Aerospace division provides technologies across a spectrum of commercial, business jets, and defense applications, making it a very well-rounded business. Based on my expectations for ~10% EPS growth annually on a combined basis due to the aforementioned catalysts, the dividend, and share buybacks, HON could produce potential double-digit total returns from here.

HON could see cost inflation from tariffs. While management appears to have a cost mitigation plan in place to counter tariffs, uncertainties in long-term tariff rates could result in margin compression. Recession risks in Europe and China could hurt short-cycle businesses like Sensing and Thermal Solutions. Lastly, spin-offs and potential for changes in valuations of these businesses are near-term risks.

Investor Takeaway

Honeywell is a solid opportunity for long-term investors seeking growth at a reasonable price. The stock remains attractively valued, especially after the recent pullback, given its strong fundamentals and strategic plans to unlock shareholder value through upcoming spin-offs of its Aerospace and Advanced Materials businesses.

HON's robust Q2 performance, increased R&D investment, and bolt-on acquisitions in high-growth sectors like sustainable fuels and building automation position it for continued earnings momentum. Honeywell is also backed by a solid balance sheet, disciplined capital returns, and exposure to secular growth markets. As such, HON presents a GARP opportunity with the potential for strong total returns over the coming years.

data.title: Unlocking The Future Of Energy Transition
data.description:
Transcript

Grover Burthey: We are pleased to be joined today by David Crane. David is the newly announced chairman of Generate Capital, the former undersecretary for infrastructure at the US Department of Energy, and also the former CEO of US Utility, NRG.

David Crane: Thanks for having me. It's great to see you and great to be at PIMCO again.

Grover Burthey: So, of course, most of your experience has been here in the US, but what do you think is interesting outside of the US from an energy perspective?

David Crane: I mean, particularly with PIMCO and the fact that you do things at scale, large scale, I mean, there's certain things that we were all excited about 10 years ago, electrification in Africa and things, which is really important.

But yeah, I've probably looked at 50 companies that have been trying to do it over the last, and no one's just figured out scale. So what I do for someone that's looking in the electricity space and wants to invest big amounts of money is say, well, when it comes to electricity, it comes, there's only about 15 countries that are big enough to really matter.

There's countries like Brazil, but it's really hard to invest in electricity in a country that's largely dependent on hydro, because if you're investing in hydro and you have a dry season, then you're hurt. If you're investing in something to compete against hydro, and there's a wet season, you're hurt. So it's not that predictable.

Grover Burthey: But within this more nascent low-carbon space, anything that you find particularly interesting or compelling?

David Crane: I think geothermal's worth looking at. I also think because electricity at the very least is going to become probably doubly as expensive as it's been, that will give rise to software and other integrated solutions that just get back in the sort of boring area of what's been perceived as boring area of energy efficiency. You know, because the electricity system in the United States works at 42% capacity or something.

And I think there's going to be a lot of solutions that just people will trend towards becoming smarter about their electricity consumption when they realize that their monthly bill for electricity is not going to be $150 a month anymore. People that are offering solutions like that, I think, are going to be in a very good place.

Grover Burthey: What do you see as potential mitigating factors to actually be able to meet that expected power demand, particularly in a world where we have potential supply chain disruption and different macroeconomic uncertainty?

David Crane: Well, on top of being green, solar and wind, those are the two forms of large-scale generation capacity in the United States that can add incremental capacity faster than any other.

If you need an extra gigawatt of electricity and you looked at the technologies, how long would it take solar to ramp up a gig? One year. Wind? Maybe two years. Gas-fired combined cycle? That's going to depend on GE building another manufacturing plant because they're sold out for the next seven years. So I'd say five to six years for gas. Coal, I'd just say never, because I just don't think anyone's going to build a coal plant, and nuclear, 2035 at the earliest.

Grover Burthey: Okay. So you combine all that, where are there financing gaps? Where is the market perhaps overlooking investment opportunities?

David Crane: A fair number of the technologies and distributed or in clean technologies are pretty highly distributed, sort of five to a hundred million dollar total project costs, investments.

And there's a financing gap there because you have to solve that age-old equation that the work that you would need to do to invest in that five to a hundred million dollar asset is almost exactly the same amount of work that you would need to do to invest in something that was $500 million to a billion. And you have to put a lot of money to work, and other people have to put a lot of money to work. So that sector, I think, has historically been a little bit underfinanced, underbanked. And again, with it being largely staying local, it's an area that I think is going to be very attractive.

Disclosure

All investments contain risk and may lose value.

This material contains the opinions of the manager and such opinions are subject to change without notice. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

data.title: Stellantis: Valuation Makes No Sense
data.description:
Stellantis (NYSE: NYSE:STLA) is one of those stocks that everyone seems to hate right now, and that’s exactly why I like it.

Let’s get the obvious out of the way. Yes, the company just posted a rough quarter. Yes, tariffs are weighing on results. And yes, they’ve got some heavy lifting to do in North America and Europe. But here’s the thing: none of that justifies a stock trading at a 68% discount to book value, let alone 4x earnings.

That’s where the opportunity is. This is a €23B global automaker with iconic brands like Jeep, Ram, Dodge, Peugeot, and Alfa Romeo. It’s not overleveraged. And yet the market is treating it like a dying business. That disconnect is where deep value lives.

Way Under Book, Under 4x Earnings

Right now, STLA trades around $9. Its book value per share? Close to $28. That’s a 68% discount to book. This is a manufacturing powerhouse with one of the strongest balance sheets in the industry, sitting on over €31B in cash and equivalents.

Meanwhile, Toyota (TM) trades at over 1x book. General Motors (GM) is at 0.75x. Even Ford (F), which has its own problems, is at book. STLA is the only one this deep in value territory.

And look at earnings: the company trades at 4.7x 2025 earnings, 3.5x forward. You don’t see those kinds of numbers unless something’s seriously wrong… or seriously misunderstood.

Yes, 1H25 Numbers Were Ugly

In the first half of 2025, shipments dropped 7%, revenue fell 13%, and operating income took a hit. Adjusted operating income (AOI) came in at just €540M, and cash flow was negative €3B.

But here’s what most investors missed: things are already turning around. Management was clear about it on the call. H1 was bad, but it was still an improvement over the second half of 2024. Volumes are recovering, pricing is improving, and a new wave of products is about to hit.

CFO Doug Ostermann said it straight:

Consolidated shipments of 2.7 million units fell 7% with declines in North America and in large Europe, slightly mitigated by growth in South America and Middle East and Africa. Net revenue of EUR 74 billion saw a larger 13% decline as adverse regional mix and lower pricing added to the change in volumes. AOI margins were compressed as the early stage of our recovery actions was negatively impacted by external factors, like tariffs and foreign exchange headwinds. Our adjusted diluted earnings per share generally track our AOI development, and our industrial free cash flow was an outflow of EUR 3 billion in the first half. While nowhere near our potential, each of these 5 figures did, in fact, improve in H1 2025 compared to the second half of 2024 as we benefited from new products, improved inventory discipline and more stable production schedules compared to the prior 6 months.

So, while YoY looks rough, the sequential trend is what actually matters, and it’s headed the right way.

Tariffs Are Real, But So Is Overreaction

Let’s talk tariffs, since that’s the headline everyone’s freaking out about. STLA expects to take a €1.5B hit this year, mostly in the back half. That’s a big number, no doubt. But the stock already got hammered for it, down more than 31% so far this year.

Here’s what people are missing. STLA is actually in a stronger position than most of its peers. About 95% of what they sell in the US is made in Mexico or Canada, and it all falls under the USMCA rules. That means they avoid the worst of the new tariffs that are hitting companies like Volkswagen.

More than half of their AOI comes from parts of the world that are not even impacted by these US tariffs. And they are not standing still. STLA has been shifting production, tweaking component sourcing, and taking advantage of the new 3.75% MSRP credit for vehicles made in the US.

So yes, €1.5B is a hit. But it is not the disaster people think. STLA saw this coming and already adjusted.

Big Brands Are Coming Back

One of the reasons things fell apart last year is that STLA had a bunch of major product gaps. Models like the Jeep Cherokee, Dodge Charger, and Ram DS were all pulled from the market at once, creating a big revenue hole.

Now they’re filling that gap. The Jeep Cherokee is back in H2 with a new HEV powertrain. The HEMI V8 Ram is making a comeback this fall, with 10,000 orders in the first 24 hours. And the new Dodge Charger hits production later this year.

CEO Antonio Filosa admitted they made some bad calls in the past:

For instance, in North America, U.S. specifically, we phased out 7 successful nameplates: Jeep Cherokee, Jeep Renegade, Chrysler 300, Ram DS Classic, Ram ProMaster CD, Dodge Charger, the muscle car of the past, Dodge Challenger, same family. Those 7 nameplates were granting to us 300,000 units per year sold and several billions of gross commercial result per year. So now we are restoring that lineup.

Now they’re reversing that and reconnecting with core customers.

A Cash Cow Waiting to Reboot

Even in this “ugly” half, STLA still brought in €540M in AOI. And they’re sitting on €47B in industrial liquidity. This isn’t a liquidity crisis. It’s a temporary margin reset, and one that looks fixable.

H2 should look a lot better. STLA is guiding for positive cash flow, better margins, and more volume. Tariffs go up, but they’re counting on new, higher-margin models to balance it out.

Also worth pointing out: the dividend is fat: currently yielding +8%. And it’s covered. So you’re getting paid to wait.

What Could Go Wrong?

Let’s be clear: just because the stock is dirt cheap doesn’t mean it’s risk-free. A few things could still go the other way.

Tariffs are already hitting hard (€1.5B this year), and that’s if things don’t get worse. If tensions with Mexico or the EU escalate, it could keep dragging into 2026. STLA can’t control that.

STLA is betting big on new launches like the Cherokee and Charger to turn things around. If those flop, the comeback stalls. They’ve already fumbled once, they can’t blow it again.

Europe’s still a weak spot. Demand’s soft, pricing is cutthroat, and margins are thin. If that doesn’t improve, it could cancel out a lot of the progress they’re making in North America.

None of these risks are being ignored by the market, I believe they’re already priced in. But it’s worth keeping in mind that this is a turnaround story, not a victory lap.

My Take

The stock is cheap. Like, this-makes-no-sense cheap.

Under 4x earnings. Under book. World-class brands. And signs of a turnaround already underway. Yes, there are real risks. Tariffs hurt. But all of that is already in the price.

The upside here is a valuation reset. Once the market realizes this isn’t some busted automaker but a temporarily out-of-favor one, the multiple can easily move from 4x to 6x. That alone would put the stock closer to $14.

I’m long, and I’m patient. This one’s a textbook contrarian play.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: INDY: Betting On India's Large Caps
data.description:
Emerging markets are an interesting source of growth, and India is one of them, with an economy projected to grow at 6.4% CAGR in real terms. Of course, this comes with a high volatility in domestic markets, but there is an opportunity.

The iShares India 50 ETF (NASDAQ:INDY) is a bet on India’s best companies, with a clear focus on financials. The fund’s focused portfolio of 50 companies and high top 10 concentration increases its focus, trying to offset the emerging market risk by putting higher weights in large companies such as HDFC Bank (HDB) and Reliance Industries (RELI)

India benefits from tailwinds to monitor, because investing in India means believing in these trends: we are talking about an increasing demand, an accelerating digital transformation, and monetary policies designed to push the economy in international settings.

A Focused Strategy With Strong Returns

The ETF’s portfolio, made of only 50 holdings, is aimed at capturing only the largest companies of the economy, companies that already are large and with a proven track record. Other ETFs investing in India like iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA), another BlackRock fund, have a broader exposure, with 158 holdings, thus a different strategy.

Financials, the biggest industry of the fund with 38% of the fund and key holdings such as HDFC Bank and ICICI Bank (IBN), both accounting for almost a quarter of the fund. Financials are a central industry in India given the country is developing fast, benefiting both from a growing demand and a growing penetration.

The Mutual Fund’s AUM in India increased 6x in the last ten years, and has only an estimated 5% penetration now. Compared with INDA, and its 30% concentration in the financial sector, we can see that INDY is not only more concentrated on top companies but also on top industries.

Smaller industries but still significant are consumer discretionary at 11%, with companies like Tata Motors a bet on the rising EV industry in India and IT at 11% with companies like Infosys (INFY) leading the $80 Bn Indian IT services market, projected to grow at a 13% CAGR. INFY is also well positioned to leverage the surge of A.I. in India, a market projected to reach $17 Bn by 2027, being one of the fastest growing A.I. market of the world.

But, looking at first financial metrics, INDY doesn't appear to be the most attractive one: its expense ratio of 0.89% is high, even for a sector-specific ETF, and more than 20 bps above INDA, standing at 0.62%. INDY’s AUM is also almost 15x times lower, at $697 Mn. The daily trading volume is also low considering the fund’s size, at $5 Mn daily. This is enough for retail investors to trade with, but considering the fund’s size, it will not be enough to cover the biggest trades.

Looking at the performance, you will see below that in the long term (10Y), both performances are almost identical, but with volatility: INDY has performed better in the short term history (1Y), and INDA has performed better in the medium term (3Y to 5Y). I say performed better, but in both time horizons, the performance actually is close and showing mid range double digits growth since COVID.

Indian Tailwinds and INDY’s Positioning

One thing to like about India is that the population is young, increasingly urban and the government is showing strong support through reforms in taxation and infrastructure, core reasons behind the projected 6.4% real growth in GDP.

The portfolio of INDY is targeting fast growing industries within the Indian economy.

For example, the energy industry (10% of the fund), with Reliance Industries as a key holding, is benefiting from the push for energy security by the government. As per the IEA, the energy industry is booming in India, investments representing 3.6% of the GDP with 83% directed to clean energy.

Consumer staples (7% of the fund), with companies like ITC LTD or Hindustan Unilever are leveraging the structural increase in discretionary spending, as the Indian middle class is expected to represent 56% of the country in 5 years.

Now the hot topic, financials. India’s Monetary policy is a key element: the country’s 5.5% repo rate, which recently was 6.5%, is designed to balance inflation with growth to increase the country's stability. I think bank holdings like HDFC will surge as credit growth increases, and as non performing loans are decreasing.

Despite this higher concentration to high growth industries, the P/E ratio of the fund at 22.71 is lower than that of INDA, at 24.72. I like this fact as it shows that the high concentration of the fund doesn't come with a large cap premium.

Risks

As you expect from an emerging markets ETF, the risk level is high.

I like the concentration of INDY’s portfolio because I’m most confident about large cap holdings in emerging markets, but this also comes with additional risks. The 57.86% allocation in the top 10, or even the significant allocation to the top 3 companies (see below) expose the fund to company specific risks.

As the Indian economy is not as resilient as the one of developed markets, macroeconomic risks are high. The country’s reliance on imported oil exposes it to the current tense geopolitical context and could impact the energy holdings of the fund.

I mentioned liquidity before, and given the size of the fund I do think this is a risk. The $5 Mn daily trading volume is small and could imply a strong volatility over buy or sell transactions by major investors.

Conclusion

The concentrated positioning of INDY, focused on major industries like financials and benefiting from structural growth (large young population accessing middle class).

This is a buy for investors wanting exposure for emerging markets, as I see India as the most resilient one.

The fund is aligned with country specific drivers such as banking reforms, digital transformation and consumer growth ; all of it coming with a reasonable P/E ratio and a competitive historical performance.

Risks are high, coming from concentration, geopolitics and the emerging nature of the economy, but I believe it is worth the potential return.

data.title: TPG: Own This Year's Private Equity Laggard Into Earnings
data.description:
From a pure-play private equity manager when it first IPO’d, TPG Inc (NASDAQ:TPG) is now a relatively more diversified alternative asset manager, with ~$251bn of assets under management (or AUM) across private equity, real assets, and credit, among others.

Still, TPG has lagged peers like Apollo (APO) and KKR (KKR) in diversifying; with about half of its AUM still in drawdown-style private equity funds and the vast majority of its income dependent on management fees, TPG remains among the more levered names to capital market trends.

Yet, even with deal activity and market valuations inflecting higher after “Liberation Day” lows, the stock has, relatively speaking, lagged its mega-fund peers. Hence, as good a time as any, in my view, for a closer look at the TPG investment case.

Stay Bullish on the Fundraising Cycle

TPG disappointed last quarter on two key fronts. Firstly, with distributable earnings (i.e., a gauge of private equity cash flows) coming in at $0.48/share – just short of Street numbers due to underwhelming net realized performance revenues. But perhaps more importantly, on new fundraising at “nearly $6 billion”, which also came in short due to “elongated campaigns” during the Jan-Mar period. The Impact platform (i.e., TPG’s group of ESG funds) was front and center here, as investment committees stay in ‘wait and see’ mode amid post-election regulatory shifts.

Still, on a forward-looking basis, TPG is in good shape. Yes, quite a few fund closings may need to be pushed out; but macro uncertainties are fading and capital markets are now much improved post “Liberation Day”. And with a slew of TPG funds set to come onto market over the course of this year, 2025 fundraising remains on track to be a lot higher than the $29bn raised last year. Key to watch will be flagship TPG Capital Fund X, the biggest of the lot this year. Having raised ~$12bn with Capital Fund IX (2022 vintage) and with historical Capital fund performance pacing at a very solid mid-teens % net IRR, I’d expect a positive update at upcoming earnings reports.

Elsewhere, expect high-performing areas like growth (Fund VI) and healthcare (THP III) to also lead the way. Inversely, expectations are low for Impact fundraising, so any positive surprises on Rise IV (note the last Rise vintage raised $2.7bn, while historical Rise fund performance is tracking at an average of ~11%) will be a big boost to the stock. Ditto for TPG’s other ongoing Impact fundraisings, including its Global South Initiative and Climate Transition Infrastructure fund, though I wouldn’t pencil in anything just yet.

The other big TPG ‘edge’ in this environment is its ability to perform while also returning capital to its investors. As management put it on the Q1 call, “the excellent DPI we’re consistently delivering” (DPI meaning actual distributions paid out to limited partners relative to their paid-in capital). This above par “ratio of realized returns to invested capital” also counts for a lot – TPG has kept this ratio consistently at >1 since 2020, meaning the manager returned more capital than it invested. In contrast, the broader private equity industry has struggled to return capital in recent years, and LP pressure is mounting; amid this fatigue, TPG has a big leg up this fundraising cycle, in my view.

Watch for Near-Term Peppertree Accretion

One key development to account for ahead of next week’s earnings report is TPG’s recently closed Peppertree Capital Management acquisition. For context, Peppertree is a leading manager in the digital infrastructure space, with a portfolio of ~8.8k tower assets and $7.7bn of assets under management.

There’s not much to fault here. Strategically, the deal ticks all the right boxes – Peppertree is a quality manager with a very strong track record (+23% gross/+16% net IRR) and a lot of runway (flagship fund sizes have 9x-ed since inception). It also ties in nicely with TPG’s push to expand its capabilities as an alternative asset manager.

The financial terms seem reasonable enough at ~$660m upfront ($242mm cash and $418m equity). There’s also a ~$300m earnout (subject to fee revenue and fundraising targets), which will, like the equity consideration, vest over the next few years, and keeps incentives aligned. Management last guided Peppertree to be “immediately accretive”, so with full contribution set to kick in from Q3 2025, expect a nice boost to TPG’s earnings per share into year-end.

Also Keep an Eye on the Distribution Opportunity

Last but not least are TPG’s opportunities in distribution, particularly with President Trump set to sign an executive order opening up 401(k) plans to private assets. For context, there’s about $12tn of untapped 401(k) money, so every incremental % point allocation to private equity would be very significant.

To be clear, TPG isn’t the prime beneficiary here – in fact, it has lagged peers like KKR and Blue Owl (OWL), both of which have already struck up new partnerships to capitalize. Still, the good early progress TPG has made in private wealth signals that the manager is catching up. Recall that having raised ~$2.4bn from this channel last year (“more than double what we raised in 2023”), TPG is pacing for an even stronger ramp-up in H2.

Key here will be early progress on its Private Equity Opportunities Fund (or TPOP), which recently launched into the private wealth channel, and is due for an update at next week's Q2 call. A good initial take-up would offer very positive read-throughs for the rest of TPG’s product set here.

Summing Up

In all, the ingredients are in place for TPG to, in line with targets, double its managed asset base over the next five years. With that scale comes operating leverage and a lot of margin expansion potential as well (>40% fee-related margin target by year-end). As for the near-term, TPG’s relatively heavy skew toward drawdown-style private equity funds means it should also lead the way as capital markets inflect higher.

Not all of these positives are priced in. In fact, the stock continues to lag its more diversified peers to date; with forward valuations also still relatively undemanding, the TPG setup looks good into earnings season.

data.title: Meta's Q2 Beat Was Just A Warm-Up Thanks To Game-Changing AI Investments
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), the stock rose 19.12%, vs. the broad market's 12.12% return. ct.

After the bell yesterday, Meta Platforms reported Q2 ‘25 figures that beat consensus estimates. Its revenue came in at ~$47.5 billion, about 6% higher than the $44.8 billion consensus. Meanwhile, EPS (diluted) for the quarter came in at $7.14, beating market estimates of $5.86 by a significant margin.

And, at the time of this writing, the stock closed yesterday at $695.21 per share (which is already 18.74% higher on a YTD basis) and is poised to rise ~11.49% - at the open.

Spoiler: I’m maintaining my Strong Buy rating on Meta - here’s why.

Meta’s Q2 ‘25 Financial Performance

Compared to the same quarter last year, Meta’s revenue for Q2’25 increased around 22% to $47.5 billion, which is driven by the 21.5% increase in ad revenue. What’s more impressive is that it surpassed the management’s revenue guidance of $42.5 to $ 45.5 billion for the quarter.

Here’s a quick segmental revenue breakdown:

Segments

Q2 '24 Revenue

Q2 '25 revenue

YOY Change (&)

Family of Apps

$38,718

$47,146

22%

Realty Labs

$353

$370

5%

Total

$39,071

$47,516

22%

Note: USD in Millions.

The segment “Family of Apps” is where Meta’s advertising revenue gets included. As you can see, both of its main segments experienced revenue year-over-year growth in Q2 ‘25. However, the Realty Labs segment declined by around 1% over a 6-month period - though I honestly don’t think this should concern investors too much. After all, the Family of Apps segment is where Meta makes most of its income.

Looking at the chart above, the U.S. & Canada remain the biggest revenue drivers for the company, followed by the rest of the regions.

As you might’ve noticed, Metas’ regional revenue growth pattern has been consistent over the past few quarters. Sure, there might be a decline every after Q4, but that’s expected given Meta’s business model - we need to take holiday spending and the Santa Claus rally into account.

With this revenue growth, Meta’s total operating income for the quarter increased 37.7% to around $20.4 billion, with an operating margin of 43% - 500 basis points higher than Q2 ‘24. However, operating loss for the Realty Labs segment increased by about 1% over the same period, and it has been reporting losses over the past few quarters. But again, Realty Labs is a portion of Meta’s overall business - it concerns me to some extent, but I think Meta is still in an excellent position.

As a result of these figures, Meta’s net income for the quarter increased 36% to around $18.34 billion, or a $7.28 per share (basic) - notably higher than the $5.31 EPS it reported in the same quarter last year. Considering Meta’s overall business profile and their size, double-digit growth in these core metrics is nothing short of impressive.

Meta’s net cash provided by operating activities also increased by almost 32% to $25.56 billion, but its free cash flow decreased 21.5% to $8.55 billion over the same period, largely because of investments in AI and the purchasing of property and equipment. Speaking of which, CapEx for the quarter doubled to $17 billion, again, driven by the expenses.

As a shareholder, I’m very pleased with Meta’s financial growth.

User Engagement Remains Solid

As you can see from the chart above, Meta’s user metrics have been consistently growing, and this has defied skeptics who claim that the company’s apps won’t be as sellable to younger generations. Surprise, Meta’s Family DAP metric for Q2 ‘25 reached 3.48 billion on average, which is up by around 6% year-over-year. A stronger user base means a sturdier foundation for monetization improvements, which is what makes this metric crucial for investors.

Meta’s ARPP itself, or average revenue per person, is the perfect example of this monetization improvement. Its ARPP for the quarter increased by almost 15% to $13.65, and has been gradually increasing over the past few quarters. This wouldn’t be possible without the consistent increase in user engagement.

Current Stock Price Details

At the time of publication, META stock trades at $695.21 per share, which is up by over 50% on a 1-YR scale, while 18.74% on a year-to-date basis. Over the past 52 weeks, the stock has traded between $450.80 and $747.90, which suggests 13.45% is the immediate upside resistance level. Since the stock is set to open around $775, I’d say based on Q2 alone, new highs will be formed.

Although the stock experienced notable setbacks during the 1st quarter of the year, it was largely driven by DeepSeek AI’s market disruption and President Donald Trump’s tariff policies. There were macroeconomic headwinds that made investors dump their high-growth tech investments and deploy funds somewhere safer - to be fair, it’s a rational thing to do as an investor, especially if you’re a conservative one.

Fast forward a few months, and investors no longer seem to care about tariffs - at least not as much as in April 2025. And it just goes to show that with Meta's solid Q1 '25 numbers, its foundation wasn’t nearly as affected by the chaos. The selloff was really just an overall transition out of tech - precisely what opportunistic investors like me are always on the lookout for. If a stock dips for no reason other than fear, you know it’s the best time to buy it. I know, easier said than done… but Meta investors were quick to notice, some bought the dip, and they are pleased. My regret? I didn’t buy more.

In terms of valuation, Meta’s FW P/E ratio sits at 27.19x, which is around 53% higher than the sector median of 17.60x. Sure, it’s a little rich, and investors are paying a premium for Meta’s overall performance. In perspective, Nvidia (NVDA) is “only” about 40% more expensive than the broader sector. But considering Meta’s performance, growth drivers, business fundamentals, and market position, I think the valuation makes sense.

After all, Facebook and Instagram hold the highest number of users, as per recent data.

While the stock is expected to hit an intraday all-time high, there could be some near-term profit-taking.

Over time, however, I’m betting that the stock will continue to go up. Here’s why.

Management

After noting the quarter, Mark Zuckerberg shared his excitement about building personal superintelligence for everyone in the world. He stated:

Meta believes strongly in building personal superintelligence that empowers everyone.

The comments were brief, but the message was clear. Zuckerberg is positioning Meta as a company that will bring more advanced AI capabilities to regular users. The phrase “personal superintelligence” is a bit ambiguous, to say the least, but what can you expect at an early stage? Are we expecting Iron Man’s Jarvis-like AI? Probably not, but the point is, we already know where the CEO wants to focus Meta’s resources from here on.

The CFO’s commentary provided additional details about Zuckerberg’s AI goals. Meta expects to spend $66 to $72 billion on CapEx in 2025, which is up by roughly $30 billion from last year. That’s a massive increase by any measure. More importantly, management reiterated that it’s NOT a one-off event - and that they expect “another year of similarly significant capital expenditure dollar growth in 2026.”

If Meta adds another $30 billion in spending next year, we could be looking at CapEx in the neighborhood of $100 billion annually. For context, this would exceed the entire market cap of many Fortune 500 companies - and many small countries! It was also said that infrastructure costs are the largest single driver of expense growth.

Remember the expenses I mentioned earlier? In simple terms, Meta is building a lot of data centers and is buying computing equipment at unprecedented rates. Should these investments fail to pay dividends, Meta’s net income will be pressured with higher depreciation charges over the coming years.

Future Guidance: Under Promise and Over Deliver?

For Q3, management has issued guidance of $47.5 to $50.5 billion with a 1% currency tailwind, which means a growth of around 17% to 24% compared to the same quarter last year. I think this aligns with Meta’s recent financial performance - it’s been delivering in the high teens to low twenties in revenue growth rates, so this guidance doesn’t really require any dramatic acceleration from the current trends.

That said, management noted that growth in Q4 will be slower than Q3 as they lapped a period of stronger growth in the previous year. No one’s saying that acceleration is impossible; rather, they acknowledge the natural cycle of their business.

Meanwhile, a consensus among 44 Wall Street analysts expects $46.8 billion in revenue for Q3 ‘25, which is about 15% higher year-over-year, and an EPS of $6.36. With how things are moving, I’m expecting Meta to surpass this consensus like how it did in the 2nd quarter. As we already know, the company’s been consistently pushing through a higher average price per ad as it expands its user base. It’s also worth knowing that the growth in ad revenue is largely driven by the company’s improved AI targeting.

With this continued momentum alone, I think Meta has a good likelihood of hitting the targets.

What Do Experts Say About Meta?

Both Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts have overwhelmingly positive sentiment for META. Over the past 3 months, a total of 68 Wall St. analysts rated Meta - about 87% of them rated either “Buy” or “Strong Buy” while the remaining 13% were mostly “Hold” ratings. There’s a lone “sell” rating. The highest price target for META stock is $935 per share, which suggests as much as 34.5% upside potential from yesterday's close.

Why META Stock is a Strong Buy

Meta has maintained its growth trajectory over the past couple of quarters and has surpassed market expectations by a significant margin. Apart from the Realty Labs operating loss, I don’t see any real signs of market erosion for Meta. It still holds the highest number of social media users, and it doesn’t look like anyone’s going to dethrone the company anytime soon. And with its continuous investments in AI, we can expect higher ad revenue for the following quarters.

As for Realty Labs - to me, this looks like a pet project that may pay out later on. I’m not concerned about it.

Overall, I’m very pleased with Meta’s Q2 ‘25 numbers, and I’m excited about how they’ll be able to use AI to generate increased earnings. That’s why I’m maintaining my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Pfizer: Lack Of Enthusiasm Shouldn't Surprise You
data.description:
Pfizer: Many Headwinds Heading To Its Upcoming Q2 Release

Healthcare investors (XLV) aren't going to have it easy, as the biopharma giant Pfizer Inc. (PFE) primes itself for the company's Q2 earnings release next week. While AI and tech investors have been dominating the positive limelight with the AI infra spending spree having attained new heights, the healthcare industry has been mired in intense scrutiny.

These include the overhaul of regulatory oversight under HHS Secretary RFK Jr. and new worries about the boom and bust of the GLP-1 obesity drugs area after Novo Nordisk's (NVO) disappointing release. How about UnitedHealth's (UNH) faux pas as the health insurer issued another slate of worrisome results and guidance update. And so, how could we blame the market for relegating the healthcare sector to the doldrums, with just over 48% of XLV constituents above their 200-Day moving average.

Hence, the relative skepticism of PFE investors heading into its upcoming earnings scorecard is comprehensible. I had enunciated in my previous Pfizer writeup about multitudinous headwinds afflicting the market's confidence in the stock. Yet, let's not assume that the market is dumb, as PFE's relative undervaluation should afford somewhat of a cushion against further disappointment at its ER next week.

Hence, I urge investors to anticipate a tough year ahead for PFE. Based on the revised quarterly estimates entering its earnings week, Pfizer is expected to deliver revenue growth of just under 2%. Hence, while the COVID bubble has clearly been digested as the normalization phase hits a nadir, substantive topline improvement may not be resuscitated in the near term. Investors need to bide their time, as we await commercialization opportunities from its burgeoning oncology pipeline, crucial to replace the potential generic revenue loss (possibly up to 25% of 2024 revenue) through the 2028 forecast period.

Pfizer: Must Accelerate Oncology Pipeline Progress

Hence, the recent licensing of 3SBio's SSGJ-707 within an exclusive ex-China framework highlights an intriguing development. While Pfizer can more than afford the total deal structure worth $4.8B (with $1.25B upfront), could it also be a solution to accelerate Pfizer's commercialization of its oncology pipeline? The company appears to have confidence that the licensing deal augments its current ADC platforms anchored by Seagen.

In addition, the economics of the deal underscores the milestone payment framework, helping mitigate downside risks. However, we need to scrutinize the progress in its global Phase III clinical trials for us to seek clarity into the commercialization timeline and the TAM opportunity on top of Seagen's current prospects.

Nevertheless, I believe it also underscores one of Pfizer's most enduring moat among peers: its robust profitability anchoring its ability to partake in BD activities to try and improve the course toward its long-term objectives. In PFE's case, the company indicated the deal resonates around a total of $10B to $15B external investment allocation that it still expects to undertake for FY2025.

Nevertheless, while these projects could help pave the way for Pfizer to demonstrate its ability to circumvent its anticipated LOE losses in the next few years, we shouldn't expect them to be near-term growth catalysts. Analysts still expect the company to post negative topline growth through 2029. Hence, unless these could result in immense breakthrough results, disappointment could persist for investors over the next few years. And that might mean that we should be prepared for a prolonged digestion period, while allowing more time for its oncology business (but, no guarantees) to become a solid long-term growth driver.

Hence, I assess that the near-term costs savings benefit ($1.7B OpEx + $1.5B COGS cuts) may not be enough to convince the market to overturn the momentum in the battered stock, since these aren't structural adjustments. Hence, with Pfizer's P&L still hobbled by a tepid topline growth cadence, investors shouldn't prime themselves for a quick (and magical) recovery, as PFE's underwhelming traction demonstrates.

Buy Or Sell PFE Stock?

What should be obvious by now is that PFE's downtrend bias is still the dominant trend. Previous attempts to rally and overturn the pessimism didn't pan out.

Still, income investors betting on PFE's dividend yield of 7% might be less perturbed with PFE's recovery. In addition, its relative undervaluation with a forward EBITDA multiple of 7.6x (compared to healthcare median of 12.3x) doesn't seem to support a bearish rating even though near-term sentiments are expected to remain tepid.

Therefore, management must accelerate its pipeline progress to contend with persistent worries relating to the LOE losses stymying its growth profile. Until then, it seems arduous for Pfizer to mount a sustained reversal against the lack of market conviction, justifying a cautious rating heading into its upcoming release.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: The Fed Isn't Sure About The Economy Anymore
data.description:
With inflation inching up in June and possible support to the US economy from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act [OBBBA], I had recently ruled out the possibility of the Fed sounding dovish in its next Federal Open Markets Committee (FOMC) statement (see section "More reason for unchanged policy rates" of this link).

That was only partly confirmed when the latest FOMC statement was released after a two-day meeting yesterday, with implications for what to expect from the Fed in September. Here, I zoom in on the statement to assess what the latest statement indicates about the reserve's take on the US economy, followed by a look at what's next for monetary policy front and the best stock investments indicated in the current phase for the macroeconomy.

Softer growth noted...

The statement starts off by continuing to acknowledge that "swings in net exports" are impacting growth numbers. But here's the twist. While in June it followed up by saying that economic activity "continued to expand at a solid pace", it now says that "economic activity moderated in the first half of the year".

This isn't completely at odds with the previous statement but also with the latest GDP report, which puts the US growth rate at 3% on an annualised basis in Q2 2025, up from a decrease of 0.5% in Q1 2025. In fact, growth is also higher than the quarterly average growth over the past year of 2%.

There are two points that can explain the Fed's stance, however:

  • Average growth in H1 2025 is at 1.25%, which is much lower than the 2.75% seen in H2 2024 and 2.3% observed for H1 of last year. In other words, based on the time period under consideration, the Fed is just stating facts.
  • Next, the pick up in growth in Q2 2025 is due to reduced imports, as the worst of the fears about higher tariffs subsided. Goods imports fell by 10.3% QoQ in the latest quarter, after rising by almost 11% QoQ in Q1 2025. This obscures the fact that "domestic demand increased at its slowest pace in 2-1/2 years" as Reuters notes.

....but uncertainty has risen for the Fed

Moving forward, the Fed continues to maintain its position on the labor market, calling its conditions "solid" and also continues to be of the view that inflation is "elevated".

However, presumably because of the latest assessment of growth, the reserve believes now that "Uncertainty about the economic outlook remains elevated". This is a contrast from the previous statement where it said "Uncertainty about the economic outlook has diminished but remains elevated."

I read this difference in wording to mean that the data, which clearly pointed towards keeping rates unchanged the last time, is less convincing this time. This can be related to the GDP report, even as labor market data remains strong and inflation inches up. It also suggests that if growth softening continues in the run up to the next meeting, the Fed's policy rate could change.

Rate cut probability rises significantly

The markets are certainly reasonably expectant of a 25bps rate cut in the September meeting. Based on the 30-day Fed funds futures rates, there's a 43.2% probability of a cut in September compared to just 8.3% for the July meeting when I last checked after the June meeting.

Interestingly though, there has been a drop in probability of a rate cut from 63.3% just prior to the start of the FOMC meeting. This suggests that the probability also reflects the Fed's position on the economy, which is less convinced of the next course of action for now.

The market decoupling continues

The stock markets barely responded to the news yesterday, with movements in all key indices ling between half a percent on either side. The NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX, NASDAQ:QQQ) was the only one to see gains, but only just, with a rise of 0.13%. The S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, NYSEARCA:SPY) and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) on the other hand pulled back by 0.13% and 0.39% respectively.

In any case, during the ongoing earnings season, it's hard to read any clear reaction because of the FOMC statement. Additionally, policy developments like the recent EU-US trade deal could also have weighed on investors' minds.

Looking at the big picture, however, indicates that as has been the case in recent months, chances are that there was no surprise in the statement, which explains the muted reaction.

Where to invest

In the time since the last meeting, investors appear to have invested opportunistically, with a rise in sectors that saw pullbacks then. As a result, sectors like consumer goods, utilities, financial services, industrials and real estate have all risen in the past month. The only sector in the red is communication services, which was the biggest YTD gainer last month after industrials.

With the Fed's cautious take on growth and the inching up in inflation rate, however, it's not assured that stocks across sector will do well in general. Here are a few broad pointers on the sectors that look good and those that don't based on my recent research:

  • Approach consumer discretionary stocks with care

Avoiding consumer discretionary stocks focused on the US market might just be a good idea, considering the expected development in the growth-inflation dynamic. In fact, going by my recent analysis on the biggest stocks in the luxury market like Hermès (OTCPK:HESAY), LVMH (OTCPK:LVMUY) and Richemont (OTCPK:CFRUY), I'd avoid even global stocks in the sector right now.

That said, there can still be some good picks. Earlier this month, I reiterated a Buy rating on cruise stock Carnival Corporation (CCL) for the fourth consecutive time since last December. Even the vacation membership stock Travel + Leisure Co. (TNL) is convincing enough to be one to buy right now.

  • Consider sin stocks like tobacco for passive gains

For investors more turned off by potential market uncertainty than sin stocks like tobacco, there's opportunity for a lucrative passive income. These stocks can steady the portfolio is there are any more fluctuations in the stock market in the near future. A patient investor can also find themselves with capital gains on stocks like Philip Morris (PM) and Altria (MO).

  • Big pharma continues to look promising

Investors interested in capital gains from safe stocks can consider big pharmaceutical stocks. Topping the list is the Danish weight loss drug maker, Novo Nordisk (NVO), which has just plunged following a sharp reduction in guidance.

Cancer treatments' specialist AstraZeneca (AZN) also offers the likelihood of good returns in the relative short-term and the immunology focused AbbVie (ABBV) looks good for an investor with a medium-to-long-term horizon.

In conclusion

The key takeaway from the Fed's latest statement is that it's less sure about where the economy is headed next. This might or might not mean a rate cut in September, though the markets are certainly more inclined to believe so than they were in the run up to the July meeting.

Keeping in mind the state of the economy from the Fed's perspective, being picky about investments could be a good idea in contrast with approach suggested by gains across sectors in the past month.

Solid defensives like tobacco and pharmaceuticals still look like good bets, whether it's for a passive income or capital gains. A more discerning look at consumer discretionary stocks is a good idea, though, with many otherwise high quality stocks waiting for better times even as some others are expected to rise.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
